headline: CORN CRATERS TO $4.56 AS FUNDS FLEE
subheadline: Grains crashed Friday while cattle surged on tight supplies and energy rallied on Iran tensions.
lead: Corn collapsed through $4.60 support to close $4.56, down 3.3% and the lowest in three weeks. The funds liquidated another 18,000 contracts as Missouri farmers finally got back to planting after weeks of rain delays. When the weather risk leaves the building this late in May, the carry trade does all the talking.
the_takeaway: Weather premium gone, carry's working, grains have no floor until new crop finds equilibrium.
teaser: Corn crashes through $4.60 as planting delays end and funds rotate out of weather premium.

one_number.unit: value red meat exports added to corn and soybean producers in 2025
one_number.context: The U.S. Meat Export Federation study shows protein demand still drives grain values even when futures markets ignore it. That $3 billion in added value comes from shipping corn and soybeans in four-legged form to premium export markets. The carry trade is pricing new crop grain, but the protein complex is still pricing the old crop at a premium.

sections.title: Grains Crater on Fund Exit
sections.body: Corn collapsed through $4.60 support to close $4.5575, down 3.3% as Missouri farmers resumed planting after weeks of rain delays. Soybeans fell to $11.77, down 1.6%, while wheat crashed 3.3% to $6.36. The funds are **rotating out of weather premium** as prevent plant deadlines approach and the Belt catches up to seasonal pace. December corn's $4.81 close shows new crop carry working at 25 cents to July, the widest since March.
sections.bottom_line: Weather risk premium gone, funds liquidating, carry spread working.

sections.title: Cattle Surge on Supply Squeeze
sections.body: Live cattle exploded 3.0% higher to $253.90, feeders up 2.9% to $368.67 as tight market-ready supplies drove the week's direct cash trade higher. The **domestic herd remains near multi-decade lows** following years of drought and liquidation, exactly what's supporting these prices even as grains crater. China renewed 426 overdue beef establishment registrations and granted 77 new ones, but the real story is supply, not demand.
sections.catalyst: Tight market-ready cattle supplies supported direct cash trade higher
sections.bottom_line: Supply squeeze trumps everything else in cattle right now.
sections.vs_yesterday: Cattle: supply story getting stronger, not weaker as called

sections.title: Energy Rallies on Iran Risk
sections.body: WTI crude jumped 3.0% to $105.42 as Iran tensions and Strait of Hormuz risks overshadowed weak signals from the Xi-Trump summit. Natural gas gained 1.3% to $2.96. The **geopolitical premium is back** after oil surged $7 this week, and energy companies are reassessing supply chains built around Middle East chokepoints. Trump's SPR refill pledge adds another bid under the complex.
sections.catalyst: Iran tensions and Hormuz risks overshadowed Xi-Trump summit disappointment
sections.bottom_line: Geopolitical premium working, Middle East risk not going away.

outside_the_pit.title: USDA Workforce Relocation Threatens Agricultural Research
outside_the_pit.body: The National Sustainable Agriculture Coalition warns that USDA's workforce relocation efforts could damage long-term ag research without a clear plan. When the research apparatus gets disrupted, producers feel it years later in delayed variety releases and extension gaps.

outside_the_pit.title: Brazilian Meat Exports to EU Banned Over Antimicrobials
outside_the_pit.body: The European Commission banned Brazilian meat imports starting September 3rd over antimicrobial compliance issues. Protectionist or not, these regulatory walls reshape global protein flows and create openings for U.S. exporters.

outside_the_pit.title: Pseudorabies Response Continues in Iowa
outside_the_pit.body: APHIS lifted movement restrictions in the 5-mile surveillance zone but maintains the 2-mile quarantine around the index herd. Disease containment matters more than headlines suggest when it comes to maintaining export market access.

the_more_you_know.title: The invisible $3 billion: why protein exports prop up grain prices
the_more_you_know.body: Red meat exports added $3 billion in value to corn and soybean producers last year, but futures markets barely price this premium. When you ship corn in four-legged form to Japan or South Korea, you capture value-added processing margins that the board price alone never shows. That's why basis stays firm in cattle country even when Chicago futures crater. The protein complex is the grain market's biggest customer, and it pays retail, not wholesale.

watch_list.desc: USDA Crop Progress: corn above 50% planted ends weather premium talk
Weekly export sales: beans below 300K MT confirms demand concerns
FOMC minutes: any pivot language supports commodities broadly
Options expiry: June cattle above $250 adds momentum to supply squeeze

daily_quote.text: Lost time is never found again.

source_summary: CME Group, USDA reports, ag trade publications, energy markets
